results.possibleIdentity: Michael Gilbert (also known as Mike Gilbert, 1947–2000) was an American artist and illustrator primarily active within the science fiction and fantasy community during the late 1960s and 1970s. He graduated from the Rochester Institute of Technology (RIT) in 1970. Gilbert was a prolific fan artist, contributing illustrations and cover art to influential fanzines such as Locus and professional magazines including Analog and Fantasy & Science Fiction. His work in the genre earned him the Hugo Award for Best Fan Artist in 1971. In addition to his illustrations, he served as the art director for If magazine and collaborated as an illustrator on children's science fiction novels, such as 'The Day of the Ness' (1975) with Andre Norton.

Michael Gilbert (or Michael J. Gilbert) was a technical professional and engineer associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. During this period, he served in roles such as Technical Coordinator or Technical Director for the organization, which facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers. He was significantly involved in the development of the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Exposition in Osaka, Japan, one of E.A.T.'s most prominent large-scale collaborative projects. His work focused on the intersection of technical engineering and artistic performance, particularly in the context of intermedia and electronic environments.

Michael Gilbert was an artist and composer active in the early 1970s who specialized in electronic music and intermedia performances. He was a student at East Carolina University's School of Music around 1970–1971, where he participated in forums for student composers and events such as 'Intermedia: An Evening of Electronic Music with Visual Effects.' His work during this time involved the use of magnetic tape and electronic synthesis, aligning with the period's growing intersection of technology and the performing arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Newell White was an artist and media maker active in Rochester, New York, during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a member of Portable Channel, a pioneering video collective founded in 1971 by Bonnie Klein that aimed to provide community access to video technology. White was involved in the creation of performance reels and video works, such as the 'Ted, Philip & Gordon Performance Reel #2' (1972), which featured music and social commentary. His work was part of a larger movement of independent video and media art that often intersected with groups like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and institutions like the Visual Studies Workshop (VSW). He is memorialized by 'Artist Park,' a space dedicated to the arts at the Rochester Contemporary Art Center (RoCo), which was established in his memory in September 1986.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg was a central figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and 1970s. Serving as the Staff Coordinator and later the Executive Secretary of the New York-based nonprofit, she played a pivotal role in the organization's mission to bridge the gap between the arts and sciences. Rosenberg managed the E.A.T. Technical Services program, an information and matching service that facilitated collaborations between approximately 2,000 artists and 2,000 engineers and scientists. She was also responsible for the E.A.T. newsletter and operational communications, helping to organize major projects such as the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka. In her own right as an artist, she created original artwork for the 1979 UNICEF 'Year of the Child' collector's plate series, specifically a piece titled 'The Earth is But One Country,' which depicted children of the world and was produced as a limited-edition porcelain plate.

Jill Rosenberg is a contemporary writer and artist who holds a BA from Vassar College and an MFA in fiction from the University of Montana. While primarily known for her literary work, she identifies as an artist who creates 'art out of anything' and has been active in the creative arts community for several decades. Her short stories have been published in prestigious journals such as the Kenyon Review, Black Warrior Review, and The Masters Review. She is the winner of the St. Lawrence Book Prize for her collection 'Now I'm Photogenic and Other Stories I Tell Myself.' She is associated with the philosopher Tiger Roholt, whom she has collaborated with on discussions regarding the intersection of writing, art, and philosophy.
